conf-proc-title-group.conf-full-title: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW)
conf-proc-title-group.conf-normalized-title: Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW), 2025 IEEE International Conference on

conf-meta.conf-name: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Data Mining Workshops (ICDMW)

title-group.article-title: Can Reasoning Llms Eliminate Conformity in Multi-Agent Systems?

aff.institution: Department of Computer Science and Engineering, The Ohio State University

permissions.copyright-statement: 979-8-3315-8132-9 © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved, including rights for text and data mining, and training of artificial intelligence and similar technologies. Personal use is permitted, but republication/redistribution requires IEEE permission. See https://www.ieee.org/publications/rights/index.html for more information.

abstract.p: Multi-agent systems powered by large language models (LLMs) hold great promise for collaboratively solving complex tasks. However, recent studies have revealed that these systems often suffer from conformity, where agents prioritize consensus over correctness. To address this challenge, we explore reasoning-oriented LLMs-so-called reasoning LLMs or large reasoning models (LRMs), which demonstrate enhanced reasoning abilities and emergent behaviors such as self-reflection-as a potential solution, and present the first systematic study of conformity in LRMs within multi-agent systems. Through controlled experiments, we compare LRMs (DeepSeek-R1, GPT-o3-mini) with instruction-tuned counterparts (DeepSeek-V3, GPT-4o-mini) across diverse tasks. Our results yield three key findings: (i) LRMs conform significantly less than non-reasoning LLMs on average; (ii) LRMs exhibit greater independence on logical tasks, but show comparable or even lower independence on contextual tasks; and (iii) stronger reasoning ability further enhances independence on logical tasks, while reducing it on contextual tasks. Overall, these findings highlight the promise of reasoning-augmented LLMs for enhancing autonomy in multi-agent systems, but also reveal their limitations on contextual tasks, pointing to the need for future work on mitigating conformity in such settings.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated remarkable capabilities in in-context learning, enabling their deployment in multi-agent systems for collaborative problem solving [1]. In such systems, multiple LLMbased agents exchange information, coordinate strategies, and collectively generate solutions, often outperforming single agents across a variety of tasks and fields, such as building robots, creating public policies, and software engineering [2]–[6]. These advances suggest a possible path toward organizational AI-the final stage of AI development envisioned by OpenAI.

Despite this promise, multi-agent LLMs also inherit a well-known pitfall from human group decision-making: conformity-the tendency for individual agents to align their responses with group consensus, even at the expense of correctness. Conformity can suppress diversity of thought, propagate errors, and ultimately undermine the benefits of collaboration. For instance, recent work [7]–[10] demonstrated that standard LLMs frequently imitate peer responses
Figure 1:
Independence rate comparison of Deepseek-R1 (reasoning) vs. Deepseek-V3 (non-reasoning) and GPT-o3mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (non-reasoning) across different number of agents.
at the cost of correctness, raising concerns about the reliability of collaborative systems.

Meanwhile, a new generation of reasoning-optimized LLMs, referred to here as large reasoning models (LRMs) or simply reasoning LLMs, has emerged [11], [12]. Models such as DeepSeek-R1 [13], trained with reinforcement learning [14], [15], exhibit significantly improved reasoning abilities, including emergent behaviors such as self-reflection-the capacity to revisit, evaluate, and revise prior outputs [16]. These reasoning-augmented models further pave the way for the development of autonomous LLM agents capable of deliberation, coordination, and adaptive decision-making in multi-agent environments [17].

These advanced reasoning abilities make LRMs particularly well-suited for data mining, where extracting subtle patterns and insights from complex or unstructured data is essential [18]. LRMs can parse, interpret, and reason over unstructured text, images, and other modalities to surface latent insights. For example, recent systems have used LLMs to build universal query engines over unstructured datasets, effectively enabling analytics without requiring rigid schema design [19]. By employing lower-conformity LRMs, individual agents can uncover novel insights, potentially transforming a wide range of sectors, including marketing, finance, healthcare, and scientific research.
These advances motivate this work to study the following research questions:

sec.title: Can LRMs (i.e., reasoning LLMs) reduce or even eliminate conformity in multi-agent systems?
sec.p: Contribution. In this work, we present the first systematic study of conformity in reasoning LLMs within collaborative multi-agent settings. We compare LRMs across diverse tasks. As shown in Figure 1, our findings reveal a nuanced picture: (i) on logical tasks, reasoning LLMs demonstrate greater independence, resisting peer influence and achieving higher accuracy; (ii) however, on contextual tasks, conformity persists, with reasoning LLMs performing similarly or slightly worse than standard models. Moreover, the comparison between DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-o3-mini indicates that stronger reasoning ability further enhances independence on logical tasks, while reducing it on contextual tasks. These results underscore both the promise and the limitations of reasoning LLMs: while they mitigate conformity in domains where logical inference dominates, they remain vulnerable to social influence in open-ended tasks. Understanding these dynamics is critical for designing multi-agent systems that can leverage collective intelligence without succumbing to groupthink.

Our contributions are as follows:
•
We provide the first systematic analysis of conformity and independence in reasoning LLMs within multiagent systems and introduce controlled experimental protocols to quantify independence, conformity, and performance across logical and contextual tasks. •
We demonstrate that reasoning LLMs reduce but do not eliminate conformity, highlighting both their advantages and vulnerabilities for collaborative intelligence.

sec.title: Conformity in LLM Agents
sec.p: Conformity is not only a well-studied phenomenon in human social psychology but also emerges in AI systems [7]–[10], [20]. Just as humans may abandon their correct judgments under group pressure, large language models (LLMs) in multi-agent collaboration can also change their answers when most peers disagree [21]. This similarity suggests that conformity is a general challenge for both human and AI group decision-making. In the context of LLMs, such behavior can undermine the reliability of multiagent systems.

In our study, we aim to investigate the extent to which reasoning-oriented models, such as Deepseek-R1 and and GPT-o3, are capable of reducing conformity among agents and fostering greater independence in their decision-making processes. By examining these dynamics, we seek to better understand whether reasoning models can ultimately enhance the overall reliability, robustness, and effectiveness of multi-agent collaboration.

sec.title: Reasoning Models
sec.p: Reasoning models such as DeepSeek-R1 and GPT-o3 are trained on specific datasets with reasoning chains, creating models designed to generate explicit step-by-step reasoning traces before producing a final answer [11], [12], [22]. This reasoning-first paradigm encourages deliberation and selfreflection, which may help such models resist conformity by relying more on internal reasoning than on simply imitating others' responses. These properties suggest that LRMs may foster greater autonomy in collaborative settings, where agents must balance agreement with correctness. However, the implications of these advances for multi-agent dynamics remain underexplored. In particular, it is unclear whether the enhanced reasoning ability of LRMs translates into reduced susceptibility to conformity pressures, especially when faced with tasks requiring contextual understanding or domain knowledge beyond logical reasoning.

Our work bridges this gap by conducting the first systematic study of conformity in reasoning models within multi-agent systems. By comparing LRMs with instructiontuned counterparts across logical, analytical, and contextual tasks, we shed light on when and how reasoning capabilities influence independence, and we identify limitations that point toward future improvements.

sec.title: Methods for Measuring Conformity and Independence
sec.p:
To systematically assess the conformity of LLMs in multi-agent systems, we adopt the Benchform framework [7], which studies how contextual information from other agents influences the performance of a subject agent. Concretely, given a fixed QA set $\mathcal{Q}$, we examine the subject agent's responses under single-round interaction protocols:
•
Raw $(R)$. The subject agent answers each question independently, without exposure to other agents. This serves as the control condition. •
Correct Guidance (C). Several additional agents answer first and always provide the correct solution. The subject agent responds afterward, allowing us to assess whether it conforms to accurate peer information. •
Wrong Guidance ($W$). This mirrors Correct Guidance, except the additional agents give incorrect answers. This setup evaluates whether the subject agent conforms to an inaccurate group consensus, even when it conflicts with its own reasoning.
For each protocol $P \in\{R, C, W\}$, let $\mathcal{Q}_{P}^{\vee}$ denote the sets of correctly answered questions. We define two metrics:

Here, Acc $_{P}$ measures task accuracy under protocol $P$, while $\text{CR}_{P}$ quantifies the fraction of questions whose outcomes change between protocol $P$ and the Raw protocal $C$; that is, questions where the agnet's answer flips from correct to incorrect or vice versa. This definition refines the conformity metric used in [7]. ${ }^{1}$ In general, conformity under Wrong Guidance reflects vulnerability to peer influence, whereas conformity under Correct Guidance highlights potential benefits from social learning. Overall, a higher CR indicates stronger influence of contextual information from other agents.

Multi-round Interaction Protocols In addition, to evaluate the subject agent's ability to make independent decisions, we introduce two further interaction protocols that involve multi-round interactions:
•
Trust Protocol (T). The subject agent participates in multiple rounds. In the early rounds, additional agents consistently provide correct answers, potentially building the subject agent's trust. In the final round, however, these peers give an incorrect answer. This protocol tests whether prior exposure to accurate peers causes the subject agent to follow them even when they are wrong. •
Doubt Protocol (D). This is the inverse of the Trust Protocol. In the initial rounds, additional agents provide incorrect answers, but in the final round they switch to the correct answer. The goal is to evaluate whether the subject agent develops doubt toward peers based on past inaccuracy and disregards their correct answer in the end.
One can also measure the accuracy and conformity for the Trust and Doubt protocols using (1). To further quantify independence among different protocols, we define the independence rate (IR) as

The IR extends the conformity measure in (1) to three protocols, but serves as the conceptual opposite: a higher IR indicates greater independence, whereas a higher CR reflects stronger conformity (i.e., lower independence), consistent with the definition in [7]. This formulation revises the definition used in [7] to capture the proportion of questions that are consistently answered correctly across protocols. ${ }^{2}$ If an agent can reason independently and reliably identify correct versus incorrect information provided by peers, then $\mathcal{Q}_{P}^{\vee}$ will coincide across the three protocols, yielding an IR

list-item.p: For example, denoted by $\mathcal{Q}_{P}$ the set of incorrectly answered questions, the conformity for the correct guidance in [7] is defined as $\text{CR}_{C}= \frac{\left|\mathcal{Q}_{R}^{\times} \cap \mathcal{Q}_{C} \times\right|}{\left|\mathcal{Q}_{R}^{\times}\right|}$, which measures the proportion of questions the subject agent initially answered incorrectly but corrected after exposure to accurate group input. However, this definition could be unstable, in particular when the base performs very well, i.e., $\left|\mathcal{Q}_{R}^{\times}\right|$becomes small, causing large variations in the computed value.

The definition in [7] is $\frac{\left|\mathcal{Q}_{T} \sim \mathcal{Q}_{D} \sim \mathcal{Q}_{R}{ }^{r}\right|}{\left|\mathcal{Q}_{R} \sim\right|}$, where the denominator only includes cases in which the agent answers correctly under the Raw protocol $(R)$. This formulation omits scenarios such as when the agent answers incorrectly in $R$ but correctly in the Trust or Doubt protocols, which still reflect independence despite failure under $R$.

sec.p: of 100 %. Conversely, if the agent heavily relies on peer input, the joint set $\mathcal{Q}_{T}^{\checkmark} \cap \mathcal{Q}_{D}^{\checkmark} \cap \mathcal{Q}_{R}^{\checkmark}$ will be small, resulting in a low IR.

sec.title: Results: Reasoning LLMs Reduce but Do Not Eliminate Conformity
sec.p: Experiment Settings. To ensure a fair comparison between reasoning LLMs and their general-purpose instructiontuned, non-reasoning counterparts, we conduct experiments with DeepSeek-R1 against DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-o3mini against GPT-4o-mini. We use both DeepSeek-R1 and DeepSeek-V3 since both originate from the same base architecture-DeepSeek-V3-Base, and DeepSeek-R1 is effectively a specialized reasoning extension of V3, fine-tuned to excel at logical chains of thought and reasoning tasks. We use GPT because both o3-mini and 4 o -mini belong to the same GPT-4o architecture family, with o3-mini serving as a reasoning-optimized variant and 4 o -mini as its generalpurpose counterpart. This allows us to fairly isolate the effect of reasoning fine-tuning within a consistent model lineage.

We evaluate each model on a total of 400 questions adapted from [7] drawn from four benchmark tasks: logical deduction, web of lies, movie recommendations, and sports understanding. The first two tasks focus on logical reasoning, while the latter two emphasize language understanding and contextual knowledge. Table 1 summarizes the selected tasks, their descriptions, and sample sizes.

For each task, every model is tested under all five protocols: Raw, Correct Guidance, Wrong Guidance, Trust, and Doubt, illustrated in the previous section. Thus, each model answers all 400 questions in every protocol. Based on these responses, we compute the conformity rates (CR) and independence rate (IR). To further examine conformity dynamics, we vary the majority number of agents (MN) from 3 to 6, thereby studying how the models respond when facing increasingly strong group consensus. In addition, within the Trust and Doubt protocols, we vary the number of prior interaction rounds known as previous discussions (PD) from 3 to 6, analyzing how longer histories of peer responses affect the subject agent's willingness to conform or remain independent. To assess the stability and consistency of our experimental results, we conduct experiment with independently three times and observe relatively low standard deviations across runs; therefore, we report only the mean values in the following sections.

Results on Conformity. We first evaluate the conformity rate of the models under the protocols described in Section 3. The results are presented in Figure 2 and Figure 5, from which we draw the following key observations.

sec.title: Finding 1: Reasoning LLMs exhibit lower conformity.
sec.p: As shown in Figure 2, DeepSeek-R1 demonstrates stronger resistance to conformity than DeepSeek-V3, with average CRs approximately 180 % lower in the Previous Discussion Rounds and 130% lower in the Majority Number

tr.td: Deduce the order of a sequence of objects based on the clues and information about their spatial relationships and placements.
Evaluate the truth value of a random Boolean function expressed as a natural-language word problem.
Given a list of movies a user might have watched and liked, recommend a new, relevant movie to the user out of the four potential choices.
Determine whether a factitious sentence related to sports is plausible.

caption.p: Conformity rate of Deepseek-R1 (reasoning) vs. Deepseek-V3 (non-reasoning) across different numbers of agents.

sec.p: experiments. Models such as DeepSeek-R1 mitigate conformity risks by weighing peer input more critically and relying more heavily on internal reasoning processes. Consequently, reasoning LLMs appear better suited for domains where agents must balance collaboration with autonomy-such as group decision-making, distributed problem solving, or contexts in which correctness is more important than consensus. This trend is further supported by our comparisons between GPT models, where the reasoning-oriented variants consistently exhibit greater independence in multi-agent settings. For brevity, we omit the corresponding figures.

Recall the definition of independence rate in (2). Figure 1 and Figure 6 present the independence rates of DeepSeek-R1 (reasoning) vs. DeepSeek-V3 (non-reasoning) and GPT-o3-mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (nonreasoning) across different discussion rounds and varying numbers of agents, respectively. These figures reveal several noteworthy findings.
Finding 2: LRMs exhibit greater independence on logical tasks, but show comparable or even lower independence on contextual task.

Both figures show that reasoning models attain nearperfect independence on logical and analytical reasoning tasks (Logical Deduction and Web of Lies), substantially
Figure 3:
Conformity rate of Deepseek-R1 (reasoning) vs. Deepseek-V3 (non-reasoning) across different rounds of previous discussions.

outperforming their non-reasoning counterparts. Moreover, for these tasks, reasoning models sustain their independence as the number of agents or rounds of discussion increase, whereas non-reasoning models steadily decline. Notably, GPT-o3 achieves a nearly 100 % independence rate across all scenarios.

On the other hand, performance changes markedly on language and contextual understanding tasks (Movie Recommendation and Sports Understanding). Reasoning models exhibit lower independence on these tasks, especially in the case of movie recommendation. Unlike in logical tasks, GPT-o3 shows the lowest independence rate among all models, with independence declining sharply as the number of agents or rounds of discussion increases. This contrast highlights the limitations of current reasoning models in handling contextual tasks in multi-agent systems, underscoring the need for future work to enhance their robustness in such settings.

Results on Accuracy. Previous results show that DeepSeekR1 and GPT-o3 demonstrate lower conformity and greater independence than DeepSeek-V3 and GPT-4o in logical reasoning tasks, ultimately yielding a clear and consistent accuracy advantage.
Figure 4:
Conformity rate of GPT-o3-mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (non-reasoning) across different numbers of Agents.
Figure 5:
Conformity rate of GPT-o3-mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (non-reasoning) across different rounds of previous discussions.

Finding 3: Reasoning LLMs excel on logical, not contextual tasks. In Figure 7 and Figure 8, DeepSeek-R1 gains over $\mathbf{2 0} \%$ on reasoning-focused tasks. For language and context-driven tasks, conformity is less distinct, and performance gaps narrow, with DeepSeek-R1 performing comparably or slightly worse than DeepSeek-V3. These results highlight a limitation: reasoning-augmented models excel at structured logical reasoning but have reduced advantage on context-dependent tasks. We observe the same trend in GPT models, though the corresponding figures are omitted due to space constraints.
Figure 6:
Independence rate of Deepseek-R1 (reasoning) vs. Deepseek-V3 (non-reasoning) and GPT-o3-mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (non-reasoning) across different rounds of Previous Discussion.
Figure 7:
Average accuracy of Deepseek-R1 (reasoning) vs. Deepseek-V3 (non-reasoning) across different number of agents (top row) and rounds (bottom row).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p:
This work examined how reasoning-augmented models, such as DeepSeek-R1, influence conformity and independence in multi-agent collaboration. Our findings show that models equipped with explicit reasoning capabilities are more resistant to conformity pressures on logical and analytical tasks, fostering more independent contributions and improving collective decision-making. However, they still conform on language and context-driven tasks, underscoring that additional advances are needed to fully realize their
Figure 8:
Average accuracy of GPT-o3-mini (reasoning) vs. GPT-4o-mini (non-reasoning) across different number of agents (top row) and rounds (bottom row).
potential. Reasoning models therefore offer both a promise and a challenge: they enhance robustness in multi-agent systems, but leave room for improvement in more nuanced domains.

Looking forward, a key opportunity lies in developing new reinforcement learning techniques tailored to LLMs, aimed not only at reducing harmful conformity but also at enabling agents to acquire more effective strategies for collaboration in multi-agent environments. In addition, future research could broaden evaluations to additional domains, investigate how independence interacts with other collaborative factors such as coordination, trust, and efficiency, and explore applications in real-world multi-agent systems where reliability is critical-from scientific discovery to democratic deliberation. By advancing both the reasoning capabilities of LLMs and their collaborative dynamics, we move closer to AI systems that can interact with humans and other agents in ways that are more robust, trustworthy, and impactful.

conf-article.back:
Acknowledgments
We thank anonymous reviewers for their helpful comments. This work was supported in part by NSF grants IIS2312840, IIS-2402952, and ECCS-2409701.
